Charcoal Chicken with Garlic Sauce and Fries

english: Half Grilled Chicken with Garlic Sauce
Charcoal Chicken with Garlic Sauce and Fries

region_of_origin: Middle East (Arabic cuisine); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Poultry — grilled chicken with accompaniments (garlic sauce, pickles, fries)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002490 represents grilled chicken food product; used as closest parent as no specific Arabic-style charcoal grilled half chicken entry exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers grilled chicken meat; used as closest parent as the full meal with garlic sauce, pickles, and fries is not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for Arabic-style grilled chicken meal as a food product.

allergens: potential gluten from fries depending on preparation

serving.description: 1 plate (half chicken with garlic sauce, pickles, and fries)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising half a charcoal-grilled chicken (approximately 350 g edible meat and bone-in portion), a side of garlic sauce (toum, approximately 50 g), pickles (approximately 50 g), and french fries (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, roasted (NDB 05013) combined with component estimation for garlic sauce and fries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; supplemented by USDA SR Legacy for fries and garlic sauce components
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Arabic-style charcoal grilled half chicken with garlic sauce, pickles, and fries exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: grilled/roasted chicken with skin (NDB 05013) as the primary component (~64% of serving weight), french fries (NDB 11400 / 21137) (~18%), garlic sauce/toum (~9%), and pickles (~9%). Per-100g values represent the blended composite of the full meal as served. This is a medium-confidence estimation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — French fries GI ~63 used as dominant carbohydrate component; blended meal GI estimated at ~48 weighted by carbohydrate contribution of fries vs. low-GI components (chicken, pickles, garlic sauce).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dajaj Mashwi as a composite meal. GI estimated using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method: french fries (GI ~63, contributing ~87% of total meal carbohydrate) and low-GI components (chicken ~0 GI, pickles ~15 GI, garlic sauce ~10 GI contributing ~13%). Weighted blended GI calculated as approximately 48, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (48 × 68.75) / 100 ≈ 33.0 g (serving 550 g × 12.5 g carb per 100g = 68.75 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from garlic sauce, pickles, and seasoning marinade
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be cautious with this dish as the combined sodium from pickles, garlic sauce (toum), and chicken marinade contributes approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1760 mg per full serving), approaching or exceeding 75% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced pickle and sauce portions can meaningfully lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load from french fries component and total carbohydrate per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should note that the french fries component drives a moderate glycemic load of approximately 33 g per full serving; substituting fries with a salad or grilled vegetables and consuming a half-portion can substantially reduce postprandial glucose excursion while retaining the high-protein, low-GI grilled chicken benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from chicken skin and frying oil, combined with sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consider removing the chicken skin to reduce saturated fat intake (estimated 2.6 g per 100 g) and limiting the fries and pickle portions to reduce sodium and total fat, as the full serving provides approximately 56 g total fat and 1760 mg sodium, both of which are relevant to cardiovascular risk management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dajaj Mashwi (Arabic: دجاج مشوي) is a classic Arabic-style charcoal-grilled chicken dish, with 'dajaj' meaning chicken and 'mashwi' meaning grilled or barbecued in Arabic. As served at Maharani Table in Singapore's Boat Quay, the dish consists of half a chicken that has been marinated in a blend of Middle Eastern spices — typically including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garlic, lemon juice, and olive oil — and then grilled over charcoal until the skin is lightly charred and the meat is juicy and tender. The dish is served with toum (a Lebanese-style whipped garlic sauce), pickled vegetables (mixed Middle Eastern pickles), and a portion of french fries. The flavour profile is smoky, aromatic, and mildly spiced, with the garlic sauce providing a sharp, creamy counterpoint. Nutritionally, the grilled chicken component is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with a favourable amino acid profile; the fries add carbohydrate and increase the glycemic load of the meal. The composite dish provides approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, 14.8 g protein, 10.2 g fat, and 12.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Clinically, the sodium content from pickles and garlic sauce warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension, and the fries component raises the glycemic load for those managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-certified in its typical preparation and is dairy-free. It is popular in Middle Eastern restaurants across Singapore and is culturally associated with Levantine and broader Arab culinary traditions.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Dajaj Mashwi?
Is Dajaj Mashwi high in sodium?
What is served with Dajaj Mashwi?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Dajaj Mashwi as a composite meal exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for grilled chicken with skin, french fries, garlic sauce, and pickles, weighted by approximate serving proportions. GI is an analogous estimate using the weighted carbohydrate contribution method with University of Sydney GI Database reference values for french fries. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data; no single approved-source entry for this exact dish was found. Values should be validated against laboratory analysis or a direct HPB/ICMR entry if one becomes available. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant portions for half-chicken meals with sides; actual restaurant portion may vary. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and are not individually confirmed from a single source entry; these values are plausible estimates. 4. The dish is classified under Singapore cuisine in the input record but is of Arabic/Middle Eastern culinary origin; multilingual names reflect both the Arabic origin (romanized Arabic) and the Singapore context (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). 5. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context (Maharani Table, Boat Quay) and typical preparation of this dish; formal halal certification should be verified with the restaurant. 6. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite meal was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
